title: 1 Chronicles 28
subtitle: David’s Charge to Solomon and Israel

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — David Addresses the Assembly; Solomon Chosen by God

refs.note: David’s charge to Solomon in Kings focuses on political consolidation (deal with Joab, Shimei). The Chronicler’s charge focuses entirely on worship and seeking God.
Moses charges Joshua: “Be strong and courageous.” David charges Solomon with the same words (v.20). The temple-builder receives the conqueror’s commission.

notes.note: “Serve him with wholehearted devotion and with a willing mind.” God requires not just action but attitude. External obedience without internal devotion is insufficient. The heart must be engaged. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: kissēʾ malkt YHWH (“the throne of the kingdom of the LORD”) — this unique phrase identifies the Davidic throne as an extension of divine sovereignty. It is used only in Chronicles. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David assembles all Israel’s officials and delivers his farewell address. He recounts God’s choice: “Of all my sons — and the LORD has given me many — he has chosen my son Solomon to sit on the throne of the LORD over Israel.” The throne is “of the LORD” — not David’s throne but God’s, administered by David’s son. The conditional element appears: “If he is unswerving in carrying out my commands... his kingdom will be established.” Then David charges Solomon directly: “Acknowledge the God of your father, and serve him with wholehearted devotion. If you seek him, he will be found by you; but if you forsake him, he will reject you forever.”

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew mālaḵ (to reign) carries the weight of divinely delegated authority. In the Chronicler's theology, every king reigns as God's representative — their faithfulness or failure directly affects the nation's destiny. The verb appears at the critical transitions that shape Israel's monarchic history.

notes.note: Solomon's wisdom was a direct gift from God, granted in response to humble petition rather than selfish ambition (cf. James 1:5). Yet even Solomon's legendary wisdom proved insufficient apart from sustained obedience. The truly wise king whom Solomon foreshadows is Christ, in whom 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Col 2:3).

Solomon's reign — in both its glory and its eventual decline — serves as a living parable of the danger of spiritual compromise. The wisest man who ever lived was undone by disobedience to clear commands of Scripture (Deut 17:16-17). If Solomon could fall, no believer dare presume upon their own strength. Sustained faithfulness requires sustained dependence on God's grace.

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 11–21 — Temple Plans Given to Solomon by the Spirit

heb.paragraph: David gives Solomon the tavnît (v.11) — the detailed architectural plan for the temple. The same word is used for the tabernacle pattern shown to Moses on Sinai (Exod 25:9). David receives the temple blueprints by divine revelation, just as Moses received the tabernacle design. The temple is not David’s design but God’s.

refs.note: “Make this tabernacle and all its furnishings exactly like the pattern I will show you.” Moses received the tabernacle pattern on Sinai; David receives the temple pattern through the Spirit.
“They serve at a sanctuary that is a copy and shadow of what is in heaven.” The NT extends the principle: earthly worship follows a heavenly pattern.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: tabnît (“pattern, model”) is the same word used in Exod 25:9, 40 for the tabernacle pattern. The verbal echo is intentional: David’s temple revelation parallels Moses’ Sinai revelation. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David gives Solomon the plans “that the Spirit had put in his mind” — for the portico, buildings, treasuries, upper rooms, inner rooms, the place of atonement, the courts, the priestly and Levitical divisions, and all the vessels of service. Everything is specified by weight: gold for the gold items, silver for the silver items. The gold lampstands, the table for the bread of the Presence, the altar of incense — every item has a divinely specified weight. David concludes: “All this I have in writing as a result of the LORD’s hand on me, and he enabled me to understand all the details of the plan.”

notes.note: The temple construction represents the pinnacle of Israel's theocratic kingdom — God dwelling visibly among His people. Yet this physical temple was always a shadow of the greater reality: Christ Himself is the true temple (John 2:19-21), and believers collectively form the living temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 3:16).

The glory of the temple — its furnishings, priests, and sacrificial system — all point beyond themselves to the Person and work of Christ. The altar speaks of His cross; the veil of His flesh (Heb 10:20); the Holy of Holies of direct access to God through Him alone. No physical temple can contain the infinite God (Acts 7:48-50), but God graciously condescends to dwell among His people.

For the New Testament believer, the application is profound: we are now the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 6:19-20). The care, reverence, and holiness that characterized temple worship should characterize our daily lives. We are called to present our bodies as living sacrifices (Rom 12:1) — holy, acceptable to God, which is our reasonable service.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVDavid summoned all the officials of Israel to assemble at Jerusalem: the officers over the tribes, the commanders of the divisions in the service of the king, the commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds, and the officials in charge of all the property and livestock belonging to the king and his sons, together with the palace officials, the warriors and all the brave fighting men.ESVDavid assembled at Jerusalem all the officials of Israel, the officials of the tribes, the officers of the divisions that served the king, the commanders of thousands, the commanders of hundreds, the stewards of all the property and livestock of the king and his sons, together with the palace officials, the mighty men and all the seasoned warriors.NIV\u201cAnd you, my son Solomon, acknowledge the God of your father, and serve him with wholehearted devotion and with a willing mind, for the Lord searches every heart and understands every desire and every thought. If you seek him, he will be found by you; but if you forsake him, he will reject you forever.ESV\u201cAnd you, Solomon my son, know the God of your father and serve him with a whole heart and with a willing mind, for the LORD searches all hearts and understands every plan and thought. If you seek him, he will be found by you, but if you forsake him, he will cast you off forever.

themes.note: David’s Charge to Solomon and Israel

coaching.tip: David gives Solomon the plans for the temple 'that the Spirit had put in his mind.' The temple blueprints are treated as divinely revealed — like the tabernacle plans given to Moses. David is not designing; he is receiving. Architecture is revelation.

prayer_prompt: David charged Solomon and Israel to seek God, obey, and build. Consider asking God about the charge you're leaving to those after you — what instructions matter most.
